sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient presents with a 3-month history of intermittent cough, initially noticed while loading horses, which was followed by a cold that led to a visit to his primary care physician, who diagnosed bronchitis and referred him for further evaluation after finding a lung nodule on a chest x-ray.
The patient reports that his cough has resolved with exercise and biking.
He has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently under control with methotrexate 7.5mg weekly, and has been a former smoker, quitting 7 years ago.
He denies any pain, shortness of breath, or other symptoms.
He has a family history of breast cancer in his mother, but no lung cancer or other malignancies.
He has no previous history of lung infections or pneumonia.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is under control with methotrexate 7.5mg weekly, and has a history of smoking, quitting 7 years ago.
Review of systems: The patient denies any pain, shortness of breath, or other symptoms.
Current medications: Methotrexate 7.5mg weekly.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 124/76, heart rate 70, respiration rate 16, O2 saturation 98% on room air, and afebrile.

Physical exam: The patient's lungs are clear and equal bilaterally, heart is regular rate and rhythm with no clicks, rubs, or murmurs, and extremities have normal skin, no edema, and intact pulses with brisk capillary refill.
The patient has a small amount of swelling in the knuckles of both hands, which is consistent with rheumatoid arthritis.
Test Results: A chest CT scan showed a solitary 2cm nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe, which is smooth in appearance and does not show any emphysematous disease.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule, which requires further evaluation and management.
Problem list: Lung nodule; suspected lung cancer; rheumatoid arthritis; past smoking history.
Plan: I will order a pulmonary function test (PFT) to assess lung function, and a PET CT to determine if the nodule is metabolically active.
I will schedule a video-assisted thoracoscopy to remove the nodule, which will involve general anesthesia, a 1.5-hour procedure, and a chest tube for 1 day.
The patient will be admitted for 1 night and discharged the next day.
I will also recommend continued follow-up with the patient's rheumatologist and continuation of methotrexate therapy.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again in a few minutes to sign paperwork and begin the treatment process.
